skill.name: responsive-design
skill.description: Create responsive web layouts with mobile-first CSS, flexible grids, fluid typography, responsive images, and practical breakpoint guidance.
skill.summary: Build mobile-first layouts that adapt cleanly across phones, tablets, desktops, and large screens.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, filesystem, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found only Markdown examples, documentation links, relative skill references, and ordinary responsive-design text. No executable scripts, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent were found in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backtick locations are Markdown code fences or inline tags around CSS, HTML, TSX, and tag examples. They are instructional text and are not Ruby, shell execution, or callable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are fenced code examples in SKILL.md. No file contains a script entry point or command runner that would execute these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Misclassified as Network Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are public documentation references for MDN and CSS-Tricks. The skill does not contain code that opens, fetches, or transmits data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear under a References heading and are not used by executable code. No network API usage was found in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Relative Skill Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The ../ paths are Markdown links to related local skill documentation. They do not read files, write files, or accept user-controlled path input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are static Markdown references under Related Skills. There is no filesystem API, traversal sink, or runtime path resolution logic.

low_findings.title: Responsive Design Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain metadata, headings, responsive-design descriptions, CSS examples, or compact TOON summary text. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic API usage is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matches are textual substrings in design guidance, not calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related APIs. Both scanned files are documentation-only.

low_findings.title: CSS Layout Terms Misclassified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are CSS grid, display, width, and breakpoint examples plus a compact skill summary. They do not run host discovery commands or collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context is static front-end documentation. No uname, whoami, environment inspection, process listing, or comparable reconnaissance behavior was found.

content.value_statement: Fixed layouts often break across phones, tablets, desktops, and wide screens. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create mobile-first CSS, flexible grids, and responsive media patterns.
actual_capabilities: Plans mobile-first layouts with breakpoints for mobile, tablet, desktop, and large screens.
Applies CSS Grid and Flexbox patterns for navigation, dashboards, cards, and product grids.
Recommends responsive image techniques using srcset, sizes, picture, and image-set patterns.
Defines fluid typography with clamp and media-query based alternatives.
Uses container queries when component-level responsiveness is more appropriate than viewport breakpoints.
Documents constraints such as viewport meta tags, relative units, and avoiding fixed-width layouts.
limitations: It provides design and implementation guidance, not automated visual testing.
It does not include a browser automation script or screenshot comparison workflow.
It assumes the project already has a web front-end where CSS or components can be changed.
It does not replace accessibility, performance, or cross-browser review.

use_cases.title: Convert a Fixed Desktop Page
use_cases.description: Refactor a fixed-width page into a mobile-first layout with flexible containers and clear breakpoints.

use_cases.title: Design a Responsive Product Grid
use_cases.description: Create product cards that move from one column on mobile to multi-column layouts on larger screens.

use_cases.title: Review Responsive UI Requirements
use_cases.description: Check whether planned screens cover typography, images, navigation, breakpoints, and container behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Mobile-First Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new page layout.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the responsive-design skill to create a mobile-first layout for this page. Include breakpoints for mobile, tablet, desktop, and large screens.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Broken Responsive Section
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for an existing layout problem.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the responsive-design skill to inspect this component and revise the CSS so it works from 320px to 1440px. Explain the breakpoint choices.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Responsive Images and Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for performance and readability improvements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the responsive-design skill to improve images and typography for this page. Include srcset or picture guidance, fluid type, and fallback media queries.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Full Responsive System
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a broad design-system review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the responsive-design skill to audit this interface for responsive risks. Cover layout systems, breakpoints, container queries, navigation, media, and fixed-width anti-patterns.

output_examples.input: A desktop-only marketing page needs to work on phones and tablets.
output: A mobile-first layout plan with single-column defaults.
Guidance to replace fixed widths with max-width and relative units.

output_examples.input: A product grid should show one, two, three, or four columns based on screen size.
output: A responsive grid strategy using CSS Grid.
Breakpoint guidance for small, medium, desktop, and wide screens.
Image sizing guidance to preserve stable card dimensions.

output_examples.input: A component looks cramped inside a narrow sidebar but fine on the main page.
output: A container-query based approach for component-level layout changes.
Recommendations for spacing and typography that respond to container width.
Notes about when viewport media queries are still useful.

best_practices: Start with mobile defaults, then add min-width breakpoints for larger screens.
Use Grid for two-dimensional layouts and Flexbox for one-dimensional alignment.
Test real content at common viewport widths before finalizing breakpoints.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on fixed page widths that force horizontal scrolling on small screens.
Do not duplicate entire style blocks inside every breakpoint.
Do not use large images without responsive sources or clear sizing rules.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete responsive CSS?
faq.answer: It can guide and draft responsive CSS patterns, but final integration depends on the project structure.

faq.question: Which breakpoints does it recommend?
faq.answer: It uses mobile defaults, then common tablet, desktop, and large-screen breakpoints.

faq.question: Can it help with responsive images?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers srcset, sizes, picture, CSS background images, and image-set patterns.

faq.question: Does it support container queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends container queries when a component should adapt to its parent size.

faq.question: Is this only for new projects?
faq.answer: No. It can also guide refactors from fixed desktop layouts to responsive layouts.

faq.question: Does it replace manual device testing?
faq.answer: No. You should still test important pages in browser device tools and real devices when possible.
